names.japanese_name: ヒドロキシプロピルセルロース
names.japanese_label_name: ヒドロキシプロピルセルロース

natural_occurrence.description: Hydroxypropyl cellulose is a semi-synthetic derivative of cellulose, created by chemically modifying natural plant cellulose with propylene oxide. It's processed wood fiber or cotton that's been chemically altered.

manufacturing.description: Produced by reacting alkali cellulose with propylene oxide, which adds hydroxypropyl groups to the cellulose backbone. The degree of substitution determines solubility and other properties. HPC is soluble in cold water and many organic solvents.

other_uses.cosmetics: Thickener and film-former in lotions, creams, and hair products.
other_uses.industrial: Coatings, ceramics, textiles, construction materials, paper sizing.
other_uses.pharmaceutical: Tablet binder and film coating, controlled-release matrix, ophthalmic lubricant (artificial tears).

eu.conditions: Widely permitted in most food categories; considered safe cellulose derivative.

japan.japanese_regulation_name: ヒドロキシプロピルセルロース

fun_facts: Like other celluloses, you're eating chemically modified wood fiber or plant material - essentially processed sawdust!
Hydroxypropyl cellulose has thermoplastic properties - it can be melted and molded like plastic, then becomes solid again when cooled
The same ingredient used to thicken your food is also used in artificial tears eye drops to lubricate dry eyes
It's indigestible by humans, passing through as dietary fiber with zero calories
HPC forms clear films when dried, making it useful for edible coatings on pills and candies
